summary: Gruber compiles over a dozen articles spanning 2004 to 2011, all predicting that Mac malware is imminent and that Apple's security-by-obscurity days are numbered. By presenting these predictions chronologically without commentary, he lets the repetition speak for itself: the same warning has been issued every year for seven years, yet the predicted malware apocalypse never arrives. The post is a masterclass in the 'boy who cried wolf' argument, using nothing but primary sources. The implicit point is that these predictions are driven more by security vendors selling products and Windows-centric pundits projecting than by actual evidence of a trend.
key_points: The same prediction — that Mac malware is about to become a serious problem — has been repeated annually from 2004 through 2011 without materializing
Many of the dire warnings originate from antivirus companies like McAfee, Symantec, Sophos, and F-Secure, who have a financial interest in stoking fear
The articles consistently frame Mac users as naive or faith-based in their security beliefs, dismissing the platform's actual track record
Gruber makes his argument entirely through curation — no original commentary is needed because the repetition across seven years is self-evidently absurd
The title 'Wolf!' directly invokes the fable of the boy who cried wolf, framing these predictions as unreliable alarmism
money_quotes: Security experts know, of course, that there's nothing magical about Macs when it comes to security. They just haven't been targeted because Windows has been such a big juicy target for so long.
This is because most Mac users believe that faith in Steve Jobs protects them from all malware.

Just as those living in shiny houses of self-righteous glass often end up surrounded by shards of their former sanctimony, so Apple Inc. now finds itself the increasingly appealing target of software hackers.
It's Mac season. The next two years will be interesting.
Of course, McAfee is in the business of selling antivirus software, so it's important to take its reports with a grain of salt.
While Microsoft's vulnerabilities might let intruders into the castle, Apple is giving them the keys to the kingdom and rolling out the welcome mat.
MAC users should not be upset about this malware news; they should rejoice.
key_insight: By curating seven years of nearly identical 'Mac malware is coming' predictions, Gruber exposes a media cycle where the same unfulfilled warning is endlessly recycled by security vendors and pundits with little accountability for being perpetually wrong.
